[0321]
The VHR after backlight load of the host mixture H1 without stabiliser can be improved by addition of a stabiliser of the formula ST and a second stabiliser of the formula la or Ib (Mixtures C1 to C4).
[0322]
Surprisingly, the VHR can be further significantly improved by use of a combination of three stabilisers of the formulae ST and la and Ib (Mixtures N1, N2 and N3).

examples: Example 1 as a crystalline solid, m.p.50 °C. 2-Butyl-5-(2-{2,6-difluoro-4'-isothiocyanato-[1,1'-biphenyl]-4- yl}ethynyl)thiophene 20 3.1: 2',6'-Difluoro-4'-[2-(trimethylsilyl)ethynyl]-[1,1'-biphenyl]-4-amine 25 A solution of 4′-bromo-2′,6′-difluoro[1,1′-biphenyl]-4-amine (CAS 2007056-39-3) (6.5 g, 23 mmol) in triethylamine (50 ml) is treated with bis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(II) chloride (0.5 g, 0.7 mmol) and copper(I) iodide (50 mg, 0.3 mmol) at room temperature under argon atmosphere. Then trimethylsilylacetylene (4.7 g, 47 mmol) is slowly added at 50°C, and the mixture is stirred at reflux temperature overnight. It is allowed to cool 30 to room temperature and diluted with dist. water and MTB-ether. The aqueous phase is separated and washed with MTB-ether, and the combined organic phases are washed with brine, dried (sodium sulfate) and concentrated in vacuo. The residue is purified by silica gel chromatography (solvent 1-chlorobutane) to give 2',6'-difluoro-4'-[2- (trimethylsilyl)ethynyl]-[1,1'-biphenyl]-4-amine as a brown oil. 35 P24-121 AT - 196 - Step 3.2: 4'-[2-(5-Butylthiophen-2-yl)ethynyl]-2',6'-difluoro-[1,1'-biphenyl]-4-amine 5 A solution of 2',6'-difluoro-4'-[2-(trimethylsilyl)ethynyl]-[1,1'-biphenyl]-4-amine (3.3 g, 10 10 mmol) in dimethylformamide (30 ml) is treated with triethylamine (15 ml), methanol (2 ml) and potassium carbonate (1.5 g, 11 mmol). The mixture is stirred for 15 min at room temperature under argon atmosphere. Then 2-bromo-5-butyl-thiophene (CAS 128619-83-0) (2.5 g, 11 mmol) is added, and the mixture is stirred for further 30 min at room temperature. Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(0) (250 mg, 0.2 mmol) is 15 added, and the reaction mixture is stirred at 70°C overnight. Then it is allowed to cool to room temperature, filtered over silica gel (solvent dichloromethane) and concentrated in vacuo. The residue is purified by silica gel chromatography (solvent 1- chlorobutane) to give 4'-[2-(5-butylthiophen-2-yl)ethynyl]-2',6'-dif

examples: Example 16-((3,5-Difluoro-4-isothiocyanatophenyl)ethynyl)-2-propyl-1H- indene Step 1.1: 5-Bromo-2-propyl-2,3-dihydro-1H-inden-1-one Anhydrous THF (1.7 L) is cooled down to -40°C, and lithium diisopropylamide (LDA, 2 M solution in THF/heptane/ethylbenzene, 0.60 mol, 300 ml) is charged under nitrogen atmosphere. The solution is cooled down to -70°C.5-Bromo-dihydroindenone (CAS 34598-49-7, 115 g, 0.54 mol) is charged to a separate reaction flask under nitrogen atmosphere. Anhydrous THF (1.15 l) is added, and the mixture stirred. When full dissolution is observed, the orange solution is transferred to the LDA/THF solution in the other flask under inert atmosphere. The addition rate is adjusted to keep the reaction temperature below -65°C. At the end of addition, the dark brown solution is left to warm up to 0°C and iodopropane (64 ml, 0.65 mol) is added quickly. The resulting reaction mixture is left stirring overnight, warming up slowly to room temperature. After 18 hours, distilled water (1 l) is added to the reaction mixture at room temperature. The aqueous layer is decanted and retained. The organic layer is concentrated in vacuo to ca.1 l thick mixture. The aqueous layer is extracted twice with ethyl acetate, and the ethyl acetate layer combined with the THF thick mixture above. Once combined, the organic layer is washed with brine, dried over magnesium sulphate, and filtered off. Removal of the solvent gives a reddish oily residue. This is triturated with heptane and the solid obtained removed by filtration and rinsed with heptane. The heptane filtrates are evaporated to dryness to give a brown oil. Further purification via silica gel P24-041 AT chromatography (eluent heptane, gradually increasing the polarity to heptane/2% ethyl acetate). Removal of the solvent from the recovered fractions gives 5-bromo-2-propyl- 2,3-dihydro-1H-inden-1-one as an orange oil. 5-Bromo-2-propyl-2,3-dihydro-1H-inden-1-ol 5-Bromo-2-propyl-2,3-dihydro-1H-inden-1-one (17.7 g, 69.9 mmol)

examples: mixture 5 or formulation of the first layer. Preferably the helical pitch in the first layer is longer than the helical pitch in the second layer. The RM compositions and RM formulations and methods of the present invention do thus allow a simple way of preparing a multilayer of two or more chiral LC 10 polymer films, by using an achiral RM host mixture comprising, or consisting of, one or more compounds selected from formulae D, M, A and T. This achiral RM host mixture can be used for the preparation of each individual layer. Chiral RM mixtures for use in the first, second or further layers, respectively, are prepared by adding different amounts of the same chiral compound to the RM host mixture, 15 or by adding chiral compounds with differing HTP to the RM host mixture. The invention further relates to an optical, electrooptical or electronic device or a component comprising an RM composition or RM formulation or a polymer film as described above and below. 20 Preferred components include a PVH, diffraction grating, PBG or Bragg PG, lens, PB lens, optical waveguide, polarization beam splitter, quarter wave foil (QWF) or half wave foil (HWF), comprising a polymer film obtained from an RM formulation according to the present invention as described above and below. 25 The polymer film according to the present invention can be used in displays of the transmissive or reflective type. It can be used in conventional OLED displays or LCDs, in particular LCDs. 30 The present invention is described above and below with particular reference to the preferred embodiments. It should be understood that various changes and modifications might be made therein without departing from the spirit and scope of the invention. 35 Many of the compounds or mixtures thereof mentioned above and below are commercially available. All of these compounds are either known or can be prepared by methods which are known per se, as described in the literature (for example in the standard works such as Houben-Weyl, Methoden der P24108 De - 141 - Organischen Chemie [Methods of Organic Chemistry], Georg-Thieme-Verlag, Stuttgart), to be precise under reaction conditions which are known and suitable 5 for said reactions. Use may also be made here of variants which are known per se, but are not mentioned here.
